step_by_step_thinking:
In electrophoresis, lipoproteins migrate toward the charged end based on their net charge. Since lipoproteins are generally neutral overall, the movement observed is influenced by differences in surface charges derived from apolipoproteins. Chylomicrons, being the least dense and having the lowest protein-to-lipid ratio, possess minimal apolipoprotein coating and thus little surface charge. This limits their ability to respond to electric fields—meaning they do not significantly migrate toward any charged end under standard conditions. By contrast, VLDL, LDL, and HDL contain sufficient amounts of apolipoproteins providing charged residues capable of interacting with the electrical field. Thus, despite variations in net charge across species, chylomicrons show negligible migration in electrophoretic separation because of poor electrostatic interactions.
\boxed{D}
**Clinical Reasoning:**
Chylomicrons have the lowest protein content and fewest apolipoproteins, resulting in minimal surface charge and limited mobility in electrophoresis. Other lipoproteins (VLDL, LDL, HDL) have higher protein content and charged apolipoproteins that enable detectable migration toward charged ends.
